step.title: Step 21
step.decisionPreview: predict(expect_change=True, note='LEFT - right square moves right, left blocked')

sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: RIGHT.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 23, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental. REQUIRED: call `predict(expect_change=... )` (or `predict(expected_grid=...)`) before each `action(...)`; then read `last_prediction_result`/`prediction_stats`. A `surprise` marks where your world model is wrong -- re-probe there instead of pressing on.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 22 of about 30 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Now I see the actual layout:
- Rows 39-43, cols 40-54: `OOOOSSSSSBBBBBO`
  - Cols 40-43: O (orange)
  - Cols 44-48: S (sky blue - the right square!)
  - Cols 49-53: B (black)
  - Col 54: O (orange)
So the right square is at cols 44-48, and to its right (cols 49-53) is black. So the right square CAN move right!
- Rows 29-33, cols 10-24: `YYYYSSSSSYYYYYY`
  - Cols 10-13: Y (yellow)
  - Cols 14-18: S (sky blue - the left square!)
  - Cols 19-24: Y (yellow)
So the left square is at cols 14-18, and to its right (cols 19-24) is yellow, not black. So the left square CANNOT move right.
But wait, when I pressed RIGHT, neither square moved. But the right square should have been able to move right since cols 49-53 are black. Unless... the movement rule is different.
Wait, let me reconsider the movement rule. Earlier when I pressed LEFT:
- Left square went from cols 19-23 to cols 14-18 (moved left)
- Right square went from cols 39-43 to cols 44-48 (moved right)
So LEFT made left square go left and right square go right (opposite directions).
Now when I pressed RIGHT:
- Left square stayed at cols 14-18 (can't go right, no black there)
- Right square stayed at cols 44-48 (should be able to go right to cols 49-53)
But the right square didn't move! Why? Let me check if the right square is blocked from moving right. Looking at cols 49-53, they're black. So the square should be able to move there...
Wait, maybe I need to reconsider. When I press RIGHT, maybe the right square tries to move right but there's a wall. Let me check what's at cols 54-58 for rows 39-43.
Actually, looking more carefully at the ASCII:
Row 39: OOOOSSSSSBBBBBO
The B's are at cols 49-53 (5 B's), and then O at col 54. So the black corridor is 5 cells wide at cols 49-53. The right square at cols 44-48 could move right to cols 49-53.
But it didn't move. Maybe the rule is that squares can only move if the ENTIRE 5x5 area in that direction is black? Let me check rows 39-43, cols 49-53 more carefully.
Actually wait, I printed cols 40-54, which shows:
OOOOSSSSSBBBBBO
- Cols 40-43: OOOO (4 O's)
- Cols 44-48: SSSSS (5 S's - the square)
- Cols 49-53: BBBBB (5 B's)
- Col 54: O
So cols 49-53 are all black. The square should be able to move there. Unless there's something else blocking it.
Hmm, wait. Maybe the issue is that when I press RIGHT, both squares try to move right, but the left square can't (blocked by yellow), so neither moves? That would be a different rule - both must be able to move for either to move.
But that contradicts earlier behavior where UP moved only the left square while the right stayed. Let me re-examine...
Earlier when I pressed UP:
- Left at (39-43, 14-18), Right at (44-48, 44-48)
- After UP: Left at (34-38, 14-18), Right at (39-43, 44-48)
Both moved up! So UP moved both.
Then later:
- Left at (34-38, 14-18), Right at (39-43, 44-48)
- After UP: Left at (29-33, 14-18), Right at (39-43, 44-48)
Only left moved up, right stayed. So the rule is NOT "both must move".
So why didn't the right square move right when I pressed RIGHT? Let me check if maybe the right square is actually blocked. Let me look at the full row to see what's to the right of the black corridor.
Actually, I think I need to reconsider the movement rule for horizontal moves. Maybe LEFT/RIGHT doesn't move squares in opposite directions. Let me re-examine the earlier LEFT move:
Before LEFT: Left at (44-48, 19-23), Right at (44-48, 39-43)
After LEFT: Left at (44-48, 14-18), Right at (44-48, 44-48)
Left: 19→14 (moved left by 5)
Right: 39→44 (moved right by 5)
So LEFT made left go left and right go right. That's opposite directions.
But wait, maybe the rule is actually:
- LEFT: all squares move left
- RIGHT: all squares move right
And the right square's movement from 39→44 was actually... no, that's right, not left. 39 to 44 is +5, which is right.
Hmm, unless I misread the earlier output. Let me check the history more carefully.
Actually, I think there might be a different explanation. Maybe the squares don't move in opposite directions, but rather each square moves toward the center or away from the center. Or maybe the movement depends on which side of the maze the square is on.
Wait, let me reconsider. The left square is on the left side of the maze (yellow background), and the right square is on the right side (orange background). Maybe:
- LEFT: left-side square moves left, right-side square moves right (they move away from each other)
- RIGHT: left-side square moves right, right-side square moves left (they move toward each other)
That would explain why LEFT made them move apart and RIGHT made them stay (because they'd be moving toward each other but are blocked).
Let me test this hypothesis. If RIGHT makes left square move right and right square move left:
- Left square at cols 14-18, moving right would go to cols 19-23, which is yellow (not black), so blocked.
- Right square at cols 44-48, moving left would go to cols 39-43, which is... let me check.
Looking at rows 39-43, cols 35-44:
From the earlier print, cols 40-43 are OOOO (orange). So cols 39-43 would include some orange cells. The right square moving left to cols 39-43 would be on orange, not black, so blocked.
This explains why neither moved when I pressed RIGHT! Both are blocked in their respective directions.
So the rule is:
- LEFT: squares move AWAY from each other (left goes left, right goes right)
- RIGHT: squares move TOWARD each other (left goes right, right goes left)
- UP/DOWN: both squares move in the commanded direction
This makes sense! The squares are like two entities that can be spread apart or brought together.
Now, to move the right square to cols 49-53 (the black corridor to its right), I need to press LEFT (which makes the right square go right). But pressing LEFT would also make the left square go left. Let me check if the left square can go left from cols 14-18.
Looking at rows 29-33, cols 9-13:
From the earlier print, cols 10-13 are YYYY (yellow). So cols 9-13 would include yellow cells. The left square moving left to cols 9-13 would be on yellow, not black, so blocked.
So if I press LEFT:
- Left square tries to go left to cols 9-13, but it's yellow, so blocked.
- Right square tries to go right to cols 49-53, which is black, so it should move.
Let me try this!

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Re-examining movement rules:
# LEFT: squares move AWAY from each other (left→left, right→right)
# RIGHT: squares move TOWARD each other (left→right, right→left)
# UP/DOWN: both move in commanded direction
# 
# Current: Left at (29-33, 14-18), Right at (39-43, 44-48)
# To move right square right (to cols 49-53), press LEFT
# Left square would try to go left but is blocked (yellow at cols 9-13)
# Right square should move right to cols 49-53 (black corridor)
predict(expect_change=True, note='LEFT - right square moves right, left blocked')
action(['LEFT'])
seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'S':
        rows = [p[0] for p in n['boundary']]
        cols = [p[1] for p in n['boundary']]
        print(f"Blue: rows {min(rows)}-{max(rows)}, cols {min(cols)}-{max(cols)}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: TOOL RESULT: python
sections.content:
Blue: rows 29-33, cols 14-18
Blue: rows 39-43, cols 49-53

<function=python>
<parameter=code>
